q: Transition elements are the metals that exist in the block between Groups II and III.
Transition elements are found in Groups 3 to 12 of the Periodic Table.
Iron is used as a catalyst in the manufacture of sulphuric acid.
Copper-nickel (cupronickel) alloys are used in shipbuilding due to their excellent resistance to seawater corrosion.
Gold is used as jewellery because of its beautiful yellow lustre colour and its high reactivity with oxygen and water.
Titanium is a major component in modern jet aircraft and is used in manufacturing artificial joints.
Cobalt is an essential component of vitamin $B_{12}$ in living systems.
IUPAC defines a transition element as a metal whose atom has partially filled $p$ subshells.
A transition element can give rise to cations with incomplete $d$ subshells (orbitals).
The $f$-block lanthanide and actinide series are called the inner transition elements.
The first series $3d$ transition elements run from scandium ($_{21}Sc$) through zinc ($_{30}Zn$).
The third series $5d$ transition elements run from lanthanum ($_{57}La$) through mercury ($_{80}Hg$) except 14 elements of lanthanide series.
The elements Zn, Cd, and Hg are considered transition metals by IUPAC because their $d$ orbitals are partially filled.
The first row of the transition elements is in the fourth row (Period 4) of the Periodic Table.
In the $3d$ series, atomic radii show a gradual increase in their values from scandium to copper.
The decrease in atomic radii occurs because the attraction force between the nucleus and electrons is stronger than the electron-electron repulsion force.
Compared to $s$- and $p$-block elements, the decrease in atomic radii for $3d$ series transition elements is more rapid.
All transition elements in the $3d$ series have quite similar radii, which accounts for their ability to form alloys.
First ionisation energies (IE) of scandium to copper increase slightly as the atomic number increases.
Towards the end of the series, zinc has a smaller atomic size than copper due to the completion of the $d$ orbitals.
Zinc has a larger ionisation energy due to increasing electron-electron repulsion as the occupancy of the $d$ orbitals is completed.
In atoms of transition elements, the $4s$ orbital is slightly higher in energy than the $3d$ orbitals.
In atoms of the transition elements, the $4s$ orbital is normally filled and the rest of the electrons occupy the $3d$ orbitals.
The filling of the $3d$ orbitals generally obeys Hund's rule, with one electron added to each of the five $d$ orbitals before pairing occurs.
Chromium ($_{24}Cr$) and copper ($_{29}Cu$) are the two exceptions to the expected regular filling pattern in the $3d$ series.
For chromium ($_{24}Cr$), the expected electronic configuration is $[Ar] 4s^1 3d^5$.
$3d$ and $4s$ orbitals have nearly the same energy.
The electronic configuration of $_{29}Cu$ is $[Ar] 4s^1 3d^{10}$ because completely filled $3d$ orbitals have extra stability.
In general, the neutral atoms of most transition elements have an essential electronic configuration of $(n-1)d^{1-10} ns^{1-2}$.
In transition metal elements, the $3d$ orbital is the outermost orbital.
When transition metals form ions, the electrons are always removed first from the $4s$ orbital and then from the $3d$ orbitals.
The electronic configuration of the $iron(II)$ ion ($Fe^{2+}$) is $[Ar] 3d^6$.
The electronic configuration of the $iron(III)$ ion ($Fe^{3+}$) is $[Ar] 3d^5$.
The zinc element has completely filled $3d$ orbitals and loses its 2 outer $4s$ electrons to form the $Zn^{2+}$ ion.
Zinc can form compounds in the $+3$ oxidation state because of its stable filled $d$ orbitals.
Copper is considered a transition element because its atom has partially filled $d$ subshells.
Copper is considered a transition element because the $Cu^{2+}$ ion has incomplete $3d$ orbitals.
The essential electronic configuration of the $M^{2+}$ ion of Scandium is $3d^1$.
Transition elements are defined as elements having partially filled $d$ subshells (orbitals).
